heroLead: An interactive web map exploring Calgary’s green-space network through parks, trails, and boundary context.
overviewText: This case study turns the map into a fuller project writeup. It explains what the project set out to show, how the interface was built, and why the controls matter for a user trying to explore spatial relationships rather than just view a static result.

meta.label: Focus
meta.value: Web mapping, GIS communication, interface design

detailSections.title: What the project was
detailSections.body: The goal was to create a map that helped users compare parks, pathways, and the city boundary in a cleaner and more approachable interface than a typical GIS viewer.

detailSections.title: What was done
detailSections.body: Hosted feature layers were combined with a local boundary dataset, then wrapped in a panel that lets users toggle layers, reset the map, and apply lightweight filtering.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: Leaflet handled the client-side mapping, Esri-Leaflet provided access to ArcGIS services, and the interface was organized to keep the controls visible without overwhelming the map.

detailSections.title: Impact
detailSections.body: The result is a more useful exploratory map that encourages users to inspect relationships between park areas and pathway corridors. <Add specific audience/result placeholder.>

tip: Tip: toggle layers and apply the optional filters to compare how visible network patterns change.
dataSource: Data sources: OpenStreetMap tiles, ArcGIS FeatureServer layers, and local GeoJSON.
